title: MABUX: Global Bunker Prices on the Rise
summary: Bunker prices have spiked, with 380 HSFO reaching USD 735.39/tonne, and VLSFO at USD 869.72/tonne. The scrubber spread widened to USD 134.33, incentivizing more HFO use, despite its higher emissions. Expect increased carbon intensity as shipowners revert to cheaper, dirtier fuels to offset costs.
content:
Global bunker fuel prices surged by approximately 30–35% over the past week, driven by the escalation of tensions between the United States and Iran and the effective closure of the Strait of Hormuz, a key global oil transit chokepoint. The disruption triggered a sharp rally in crude oil markets, pushing major benchmarks to multi-year highs and significantly increasing volatility across the global energy complex. Against this backdrop, bunker fuel indices recorded substantial gains across all segments. The MABUX Global 380 HSFO Index rose by USD 159.82, increasing from USD 575.57/MT last week to USD 735.39/MT. The VLSFO Index posted an even stronger advance, climbing by USD 199.41, from USD 670.31/MT to USD 869.72/MT. The MGO LS Index also experienced a sharp increase, rising by USD 186.34, from USD 1043.18/MT last week to USD 1229.52/MT. During the week, the index briefly exceeded the USD 1300.00/MT level, marking the highest value recorded since the start of MABUX data tracking in 2001. At the time of writing, the global bunker market has resumed its upward trajectory, reflecting persistent uncertainty in oil supply routes and continued pressure on energy prices.

The MABUX Global Scrubber Spread (SS) — the price differential between 380 HSFO and VLSFO — increased sharply during the week, rising by USD 39.59, from USD 94.74 last week to USD 134.33. The spread significantly exceeded the psychological threshold of USD 100.00 (SS Breakeven), indicating a notable shift in the relative economics of scrubber-equipped vessels. The weekly average of the global SS index also increased by USD 9.71. In Rotterdam, the SS Spread rose by USD 10.00, from USD 38.00 last week to USD 48.00, remaining close to the USD 50.00 level. At the same time, the weekly average SS Spread in the port decreased by USD 2.66, reflecting uneven price dynamics during the week. In Singapore, the 380 HSFO/VLSFO differential recorded the most significant increase, driven by an acute fuel shortage at the hub. The spread surged by USD 133.00, from USD 72.00 last week to USD 205.00, at one point briefly reaching USD 250.00. The weekly average SS Spread in Singapore rose by USD 73.33, highlighting the severity of supply disruptions in the region. Overall, the SS Spread ended the week sharply higher, primarily influenced by fuel supply disruptions and heightened volatility amid the escalating conflict in the Middle East. Given the current market conditions, the SS Spread may continue to strengthen in the coming week. More detailed information is available in the “Differentials” section on mabux.com.

At the end of the week, the ECA Spread (ES) in Istanbul recorded a decline of USD 75.00, falling from USD 225.00 last week to USD 150.00. However, amid the high volatility in the bunker market, the ES temporarily surged to peak levels of around USD 400.00 during the week. At the same time, the weekly average value of the index increased by USD 123.33, reflecting sharp fluctuations in distillate prices. In Venice, the ECA Spread remained at USD 105.00. However, due to the lack of regular quotations caused by limited fuel availability, this level should be considered indicative rather than fully representative of actual market conditions. The Istanbul ES index reflects a sharp increase in marine gasoil prices in the regional bunker market, driven by escalating military tensions in the Middle East, which have significantly affected supply dynamics and price formation. Given the current market environment, we believe the ECA Spread may retain upward potential in the coming week.

Military escalation in the Middle East and the effective closure of the Strait of Hormuz have triggered a sharp reaction in European gas markets, with the Title Transfer Facility (TTF) benchmark rising significantly amid concerns over potential disruptions to global liquefied natural gas (LNG) flows. The shock has quickly transmitted into European power markets, where price movements have diverged depending on the degree of gas exposure within national electricity generation mixes. Beyond the immediate volatility, the market response once again highlights Europe’s structural vulnerability stemming from its continued reliance on imported fossil fuels exposed to geopolitical risk. In this context, the latest developments reinforce the strategic argument for accelerating the energy transition and further diversifying the continent’s energy supply structure.

As of 10 March, the European underground gas storage level continued to decline, reaching 29.29% of total capacity, down 0.60 percentage points compared to the previous week. Overall, storage levels are now 32.17 percentage points lower than on 1 January 2026, when inventories stood at 61.46%. After a sharp increase at the beginning of last week, the European TTF gas benchmark recorded a moderate decline in week 11, falling by €6.897/MWh, from €54.290/MWh the previous week to €47.393/MWh. Despite this correction, TTF prices remained close to the €50/MWh level.

LNG bunker prices at the port of Sines (Portugal) recorded a sharp increase this week, rising by USD 764.00 to USD 1,538/MT, compared with USD 774/MT the previous week. As a result, the price differential between LNG and conventional marine fuel shifted in favor of conventional fuel. The spread reached USD 188 in favor of MGO LS, whereas a week earlier LNG maintained an advantage of USD 165. On March 9, the price of MGO LS at the port of Sines was quoted at USD 1,350/MT. More detailed information is available in the “LNG Bunkering” section at mabux.com.

Amid heightened volatility in the global bunker market and the ongoing escalation of tensions in the Middle East, the MABUX Market Differential Index (MDI) — which reflects the ratio between market bunker prices (MBP) and the MABUX digital bunker benchmark (DBP) — shifted into the overvaluation zone across the world’s major bunkering hubs, including Rotterdam, Singapore, Fujairah, and Houston:

• 380 HSFO segment: Overvaluation levels increased significantly across all ports: by 66 points in Rotterdam, 230 points in Singapore, 159 points in Fujairah, and 83 points in Houston. Notably, the MDI values in Singapore and Fujairah moved well above the USD 100.00 threshold, indicating a substantial divergence between market prices and the MABUX digital benchmark.

• VLSFO segment: The average overvaluation premium also rose markedly: by 55 points in Rotterdam, 289 points in Singapore, 125 points in Fujairah, and 42 points in Houston. As in the HSFO segment, Singapore and Fujairah MDIs significantly exceeded the USD 100.00 level, reflecting strong upward pressure on market prices.

• MGO LS segment: All ports moved into the overvalued zone, with the exception of Rotterdam, which remained the only undervalued port in this segment. However, the undervaluation discount in Rotterdam narrowed by 7 points. In contrast, the average overvaluation premium increased sharply in the other hubs: by 418 points in Singapore, 156 points in Fujairah, and 150 points in Houston.

The rapid increase in bunker fuel overvaluations across all segments was primarily driven by an emerging supply shortage in regional bunker markets, amplified by geopolitical tensions and disruptions in energy supply chains. Given the current market dynamics, overvaluation is likely to continue dominating the global bunker market in the near term. More detailed information on the correlation between market bunker prices and the MABUX digital benchmark is available in the “Digital Bunker Prices” section on mabux.com.

A group of 87 companies representing shipowners, ports, energy producers and technology suppliers has issued a joint statement urging the International Maritime Organization (IMO) to adopt the Net-Zero Framework (NZF) within the current year. The signatories describe the NZF as a balanced and pragmatic pathway for advancing the decarbonization of international shipping. Among the supporting organizations are shipowners CMB.Tech, DFDS, Klaveness Combination Carriers, Pacific Basin Shipping, Samskip, VEER Group and X-Press Feeders. The statement is also backed by several major port authorities, including the Ports of Antwerp-Bruges, Bremen and Esbjerg, as well as energy producers Moeve, Yara International and LONGi. The proposed NZF would introduce progressively tightening limits on the greenhouse gas (GHG) intensity of energy used by ships. Compliance would be enforced through a tiered system of penalties applied to vessels that fail to meet the established thresholds. The regulatory proposal was initially endorsed by IMO member states during the Marine Environment Protection Committee meeting (MEPC 83) in April last year, passing with a 63–16 vote. However, an extraordinary session scheduled for October to formally adopt the framework was postponed, delaying final approval for one year. The issue is expected to return to the agenda at the upcoming MEPC 84 meeting in late April. During this session, member states may submit proposals to amend elements of the Net-Zero Framework or provide additional clarification through implementation guidelines.
